Tafseer:
The lesson from opposites and their mutual harmony:
This draws attention to some of the prominent signs of the universe that exist as pairs—namely, having the status of mates. The arguments Allah has established for monotheism, the Resurrection, and recompense from the fact that He has created everything in pairs have been explained in the previous Surahs. The specific aspect to which He draws attention here is: although there is a vast difference in the shape, appearance, manifestations, temperaments, and effects of the sun, moon, night, and day—making this world appear as a battlefield of opposites—the Controller of the universe has fitted these opposites into the machinery of this world in such a manner that they never clash. Rather, they cooperate with complete harmony in their respective orbits for the collective benefit of the universe. The sun does not trespass into the boundaries of the moon, the moon does not hasten to appear before its time, the day does not have the power to appear before its turn, and the night does not dare to dismiss the day before it completes its duty:
(It is not allowable for the sun to reach the moon, nor does the night outrun the day) (Ya-Sin, 36:40).
It is this mutual harmony of opposites upon which the survival of the universe depends. If rebellion and defiance were to develop in them instead of this compliance, this world would be devastated in the twinkling of an eye. For this reason, the Creator of the universe has bound them to their boundaries, and they bear witness to the inhabitants of the earth that they too should observe the limits He has established. If they violate His limits, they will cause corruption on earth, and the Lord of the earth will not tolerate those who cause corruption in His kingdom.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 1, 2, 3, and 4 was combined in the original text.]
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 1, 2, 3, and 4 was combined in the original text.]
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 1, 2, 3, and 4 was combined in the original text.]

The lesson for man in the construction of heaven and earth:
This draws attention to the construction, grandeur, and benefit of the heaven and earth—showing that they also bear witness to their Creator's great power, limitless wisdom, and all-encompassing providence. No task is impossible for Him, His wisdom is deep, and His mercy and providence are universal. The requirement of His power, wisdom, and providence is that He should not leave people unbridled in this world, but should see what those for whom He created all of this are doing, and then recompense them in accordance with their deeds. If He did not do so, all His power, wisdom, providence, and mercy would be meaningless, and His entire creation would be a vain work.
Whether 'ma' is relative (mawsoolah) or infinitive (masdariyah): A question arises regarding 'wa ma banaha' (and He Who constructed it) and 'wa ma tahaha' (and He Who spread it)—namely: is 'ma' relative or infinitive? In our view, it is infinitive. The reason is: if we regard it as relative, we would have to assume it refers to God, whereas these oaths are not by God Himself, but by His signs of power and wisdom, specifically those aspects that inspire the reflection necessary to accept the teachings of this Surah. The preceding oaths are by the sun, moon, night, and day, accompanied by the conditions 'idh tala-ha' (when it follows it), 'idh jalla-ha' (when it brightens it), 'idh yaghsha-ha' (when it covers it), to direct the gaze correctly. In this context, if He were to state: 'and by the heaven and by Allah Who constructed it,' the nature of this oath would be completely different from the preceding ones, as one part would serve as a testimony and the other as praise and sanctification, which has no place here. Here, Allah Almighty has presented the testimony of His signs, not the testimony of His self.
Furthermore, 'ma' is not suitable for referring to Allah Almighty.
Regarding 'ma' as infinitive, remember that it does not merely turn the verb into a verbal noun, but draws attention to the power, majesty, wisdom, benefit, uniqueness, and craftsmanship manifest in that verb. For example, regarding the heaven, 'wa ma banaha' means: 'and by the heaven and its amazing construction,' encompassing all the wonders of the heaven through which the Qur'an has argued for its various claims. Obviously, 'ma' as relative does not possess the capacity to point to these argumentative aspects. Because of this comprehensiveness, translating 'ma' as infinitive into another language is extremely difficult. Some translators have tried to convey its meaning, but because this style is not common in Urdu, the full meaning could not be conveyed. I have also tried, but I confess my inability to fulfill its right.
Try to understand 'And [by] the earth and He Who spread it' (wa-l-ardi wa ma tahaha) in this light. In Surah Al-Ghashiyah, He stated: (And at the earth—how it is spread?) (88:20), and we clarified that this brief statement encompasses all the details of the earth's signs explained in other places of the Qur'an to argue for His claims. Thus, what was described in Surah Al-Ghashiyah through 'how' (kayfa) is described here through 'ma' as infinitive. However, there is a subtle difference in their usage, which cannot be discussed here.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 5 and 6 was combined in the original text.]

The internal proof after the signs of nature:
Following the signs of nature, this draws attention to the internal proof: if man reflects on his own soul, it will become clear that the Creator structured his soul in such a manner that He endowed it with the awareness of both goodness and evil. This awareness was obviously endowed so that man would choose goodness and avoid evil, showing that success belongs only to those who purify themselves from evil, and ruin is for those who corrupt themselves with sins. In other words, regarding yourself as an unbridled beast is contrary to the testimony of your own soul.
The indefinite form of 'nafs' (soul) can point to insignificance, significance, or abundance, but in my view, it is to highlight its high status. Examples of this have appeared previously—such as in Surah Al-Buruj: (And [by] the witness and the witnessed) (verse 3), and Surah Al-Balad: (And [by] the father and what he begot). Their explanations were presented. Similarly, here we have: (And [by] the soul and He who proportioned it) (wa nafsin-wa ma sawwaha), which draws attention to the wise structure and superior physical and spiritual capabilities of the human soul.
In 'wa ma sawwaha', 'ma' is infinitive, drawing attention to the wise structure and proportioning of the human soul—showing that He could not have created this masterpiece with such high capabilities in vain; therefore, it is inevitable that one Day, it will be questioned regarding its capabilities and His blessings.
The word 'taswiyah' (proportioning) was discussed: it refers to the final stage of creation, as stated:
(Who created and proportioned [sawwa]) (Al-Alaq, 87:2).
This reveals that the oath here does not concern merely the initial stage of creation, but the final stage when it appeared as a masterpiece of His power, bearing witness that it was created with responsibilities. In this, man is God's representative and is accountable before Him.

'And inspired it [with discernment of] its wickedness and its righteousness' (fa-alhamaha fujooraha wa taqwaha) - This is the detail of the proportioning. The final stage of human creation is that Allah Almighty endowed him with a divine light, which awakened in him the awareness of what is goodness and what is evil. In the previous Surah, this was pointed to as: (And shown him the two ways) (Al-Balad, 90:10); refer to its explanation for details, and refer also to Surah Al-Qiyamah and Surah Ad-Dahr.

'He has succeeded who purifies it, and he has failed who instills it [with corruption]' (qad aflaha man zakkaha, wa qad khaba man dassaha) - This is the natural and obvious requirement of the awareness of goodness and evil: when Allah Almighty has blessed man with the capacity to distinguish between them, he is obligated to choose goodness and avoid evil. This is the only path that opens the way of success in this world and the Hereafter. If he chooses evil and corruption, this will be the cause of his misery and ruin.
'Dassaha' is originally 'dassaha' from the root 'dasas', which means burying something in the dust. Through a grammatical change, it became 'dassaha' to express intensity—meaning: he buried it completely in the dust. Examples of this exist in Arabic, such as 'tadannana' becoming 'tadanna'.
We regarded this as the natural requirement of the awareness of goodness and evil because whatever blessing Allah Almighty bestows on His servant, the servant is obligated to use it for its correct purpose. This is the only way to benefit, and is indeed the true gratitude for that blessing. If he fails to do so, he destroys himself. For example, one whom God has blessed with two eyes is obligated to walk with open eyes to see the obstacles on the path. If he walks with closed eyes, his falling into a pit is inevitable, and the responsibility will lie solely on him.
What is the subject of these oaths? A question arises: what is the subject of these oaths? Some have regarded 'He has succeeded who purifies it...' as the subject, but Al-Zamakhshari disagreed. In our view, his disagreement is correct. The oaths by the sun, moon, night, and day, as explained, point to the reality that the reins of all elements are in the hand of His power, Who does not allow them to deviate, lest the opposites clash and destroy the world.
Next, the oaths by the heaven and earth point to His power, wisdom, and providence—showing that His attributes require Him not to leave man unbridled, but He must inevitably bring a Day of account. This is the requirement of His power, wisdom, and providence.
Third, the oath by the creation of the human soul is an internal proof—showing that since the Creator has endowed man's nature with the awareness of goodness and evil, success belongs only to those who choose goodness, and ruin is for those who choose evil.
This reveals that 'He has succeeded who purifies it...' is not the subject of the oaths, but explains a specific aspect of the final oath. Since the final part points to the subject, the subject was omitted, which Al-Zamakhshari preferred. Examples of this omission have appeared previously. The subject can be summarized as: 'The Creator of the universe does not tolerate a nation's transgression, but He inevitably destroys them.'
Recall the Qur'an's philosophy of history—showing that nations are punished for their transgression as a group in this world, whereas in the Hereafter, individuals will be judged in their individual capacity, and everyone will receive reward or punishment according to their deeds.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 9 and 10 was combined in the original text.]

A historical testimony:
Following the signs of nature and the internal proof, this historical testimony is presented to prove the claim: a nation that transgresses is inevitably destroyed by Allah Almighty after His proof is established. Since signs in nature and the internal proof require reflection, they are useful for the rational, but do not affect common people in the same manner. For common people, historical testimonies are more effective, provided they possess some capacity. Therefore, the Qur'an has presented historical testimonies (footnote 1) alongside the signs of nature and the internal proof to establish the proof fully.
While other places of the Qur'an mention multiple nations, here reference is made specifically to one nation—Thamud. There are some clear reasons for this.
Some reasons for mentioning Thamud specifically: One reason is that among the ancient nations, the Quraysh were most familiar with their history and end. Our teacher explained their conditions and their similarity to the Quraysh in detail. We present the essential part of his explanation:
'The Allah has presented before the Arabs the history of those nations with whom they were well acquainted. It is incorrect to assume that the mention of "Thamud denied" was as vague to the Quraysh as it is to us today. The indications in this Surah were sufficient to bring their entire history before the Quraysh. They belonged to the ancient Arabs whose settlements and traditions were inherited by the Arabs. Poetic verses and proverbs concerning them were common among them, and the Qur'an itself is the greatest proof.'
After citing the Qur'anic proofs, he wrote:
'The poets also mentioned them as a well-known nation... Their power and majesty were proverbial. Khansa wrote:
"And he met from the days a day,
just as before, Qidar was not spared"
where "Qidar" refers to the leader of Thamud who killed the she-camel. Just as Qayl bin Amr was a rebellious leader of Aad, Qidar was a rebellious and absolute leader of Thamud. The famous pre-Islamic poet, Al-Afwah al-Awdi, compared the wicked of his tribe to Qayl and Qidar in a poem:
"Among us are groups who built nothing for their nation,
and if their nation built what they corrupted, they corrupted it again."
"They do not seek guidance, nor listen to their guide;
ignorance and deviation are found together in them."
"They are like Qayl bin Amr among his people,
under whose deeds Aad was destroyed,"
"Or like Qidar after him, whose followers
chose deviation and were destroyed."' 
This shows that the rebelliousness of Thamud, the deviation of their leaders, and their exemplary end were well known to the Arabs, and their poets used them as proverbs in their verses; hence, the brief reference in the Qur'an was sufficient to bring the entire detail of their transgression and its end before them.
Note the word 'taghwa' in 'Thamud denied [their messenger] by reason of their transgression' (kadhdhabat Thamudu bi-taghwaha). It means rebelliousness and open defiance of Allah's limits—specifically, the rebelliousness a nation commits when the truth has been made entirely clear to them. This word is related to the core theme of the Surah, showing that Thamud did not reject their messenger because the truth was not clear to them, but because they chose rebelliousness despite the truth being clear.
_____
Footnote 1: Historical testimonies belong to the category of signs of nature, but I have mentioned them separately here due to their specific importance.

'When the most wretched of them was sent forth' (idh-imba'atha ashqaha) - This details their transgression. 'The most wretched' refers to their leader, Qidar, whose wretchedness led to the destruction of the entire nation. 'Imbi'ath' means rising and committing himself to a task, referring here to his committing himself to the crime that opened the doors of divine wrath on his nation. The details appeared in the previous Surahs. (Footnote 1) When Prophet Salih warned his people of the punishment, they demanded a sign of this punishment before they would believe. Salih nominated a she-camel as the sign of punishment, warning them: 'If you harm her, the punishment will befall you.' He also established a test: she would drink from the well on one day, and they would drink on the other. They refused to observe this restriction, and protested to their leader, who rose and hamstrung the she-camel. Allah Almighty granted them three days' respite to repent, but they grew even more arrogant, and were destroyed.
_____
Footnote 1: Refer to the commentary of Surah Al-Qamar, verses 27-29.

'And the messenger of Allah said to them, "[Do not harm] the she-camel of Allah or her drink"' (fa-qala lahum rasulu-llahi naqata-llahi wa suqyaha) - When Salih saw that they were determined to commit the crime, he gave them a final warning: 'Beware of the she-camel of Allah and her turn to drink, lest the punishment befall you.'
'Naqata-llah' is in the accusative for warning (tahdheer), meaning: 'Beware of...' Omiting the verb is to focus the listener's entire attention on the warning, which is common in every language.

Tahbseer:
'But they denied him and hamstrung her' (fa-kadhdhabuhu fa-'aqaruha) - That is: just as they rejected the threat of punishment previously, they ignored this final warning of the Messenger, rejecting it as a mere threat, and did what they intended.
'Aqr' means hamstringing a camel's legs, which inevitably leads to its death; hence, it is used in the sense of killing, but its root meaning is what we noted.
A point regarding the Qur'an's philosophy of history: It should noted that although the crime was committed by a single individual, Allah Almighty held the entire nation guilty and punished them all. This shows that Allah Almighty punishes the entire nation for the crime of an individual if the nation approves of it. Only those are saved from its consequence who do everything they can to correct the situation, or at least loathe and separate from it, which is the minimum requirement of faith; there is no other way to escape His grasp.
The nature of the punishment of Thamud: 'So their Lord brought down upon them destruction for their sin' (fa-damdama 'alayhim rabbuhum-bi-dhambihim) - 'Damdama' means to destroy, but contains the meaning of the intensity and irresistible nature of the punishment. It means: 'their Lord poured down upon them destruction'. The same was expressed elsewhere as: (And your Lord poured upon them a scourge of punishment) (Al-Fajr, 89:13). We explained under Surah Al-Qamar, verse 37, that the punishment they faced was a combination of winter clouds, hail, thunder, lightning, and storm winds, for which 'damdama' is highly appropriate.
The she-camel was the sign of punishment: 'for their sin' (bi-dhambihim) - That is: this punishment befell them because they hamstrung the she-camel despite the warning. The she-camel was the sign of punishment, serving as a test of their transgression. If they had been granted respite after this crime, they would have dared to attack the Messenger himself, and Allah does not grant respite to a nation when they intend to kill His messenger; they are inevitably destroyed.
Pointing to the secret designs of the Quraysh: It appears this Surah was revealed when the leaders of the Quraysh had begun to consult in Dar al-Nadwah regarding killing the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him). Since these consultations were secret, the Qur'an warned them in the language of indications to reflect on the consequences of their evil designs.
'And made it equal' (fa-sawwaha) - That is: Allah Almighty poured down His punishment on them, erasing them completely. The reference of the pronoun is Thamud and their land.
'Neither the rare remained, nor the unique!'

Tafseer:
Allah has no fear of any consequence:
That is: when Allah Almighty destroys a nation in this manner, He does so in accordance with His established law of wisdom and power; therefore, He has no fear of making any mistake in His decision, nor does He fear being challenged by anyone. He is neither accountable to anyone, nor can anyone overcome Him.
This refutes those myths introduced into the Book of Genesis by its narrators—for example:
'And the Lord saw that the wickedness of man was great in the earth, and that every imagination of the thoughts of his heart was only evil continually. And it repented the Lord that he had made man on the earth, and it grieved him at his heart.' (Genesis, 6:5-6).
And after the flood of Noah:
'And the Lord smelled a sweet savor; and the Lord said in his heart, I will not again curse the ground any more for man's sake; for the imagination of man's heart is evil from his youth; neither will I again smite any more every thing living, as I have done.' (Genesis, 8:21).
